item.title: Court to rule on governorship row
item.description: THE National Court is expected to make a ruling on Friday on the governorship of New Ireland – while restraining provincial seat MP Byron Chan and Namatanai MP Walter Schnaubelt from occupying that office. Judge Pauline Bre yesterday in Waigani restrained Chan and Schnaubelt from performing and excising the powers and functions as New Ireland governor until further orders of the court.
item.content:encoded:
THE National Court is expected to make a ruling on Friday on the governorship of New Ireland – while restraining provincial seat MP Byron Chan and Namatanai MP Walter Schnaubelt from occupying that office.

Judge Pauline Bre yesterday in Waigani restrained Chan and Schnaubelt from performing and excising the powers and functions as New Ireland governor until further orders of the court. 

Chan’s lawyer Alfred Manase had sought a court declaration that Chan was the governor of New Ireland after winning the recent by-election for that seat left vacant by his late father Sir Julius Chan.

Manase submitted that Section 101 of the Constitution clarifies that no MP could represent two or more electorates at the same time.

He added that Section 104 (g) explains that the seat of a Member of the Parliament becomes vacant on his death.

Section 106 provides for a by-election to fill the vacancy.

He argued that Chan was the governor because of Section 17(2) of the Organic Law on Provincial Government and Local Level Government (OLPGLLG).

That issue was determined in Launa v Willie OS 402 of 2004, and Wai v Ambane OS501 of 1998.

Manase said Section 19 dismissed Schnaubelt, who was elected by the provincial assembly, from office as there was no longer a vacancy existing following Chan’s by-election victory.

Schnaubelt’s lawyer Rimbink Pato said there were separate offices under the law, and the matter before the court was for the provincial assembly to decide on.

He said that in Mango v Muthuvel 2019 N7916, whether it was by death or whenever a vacancy arose, the person appointed by the provincial assembly as governor should remain in office.

Judge Bre told the parties that she needed time to go through the submissions before making a ruling.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Six Boram Airport raid suspects in custody: Police
item.description: SIX men believed to be involved in a raid on Boram Airport in Wewak, East Sepik, on Friday night are in police custody. Provincial police commander Supt Christopher Tamari confirmed that the arrests were made during a joint police-army operation in Malasi and nearby settlements on Saturday.
item.content:encoded:
SIX men believed to be involved in a raid on Boram Airport in Wewak, East Sepik, on Friday night are in police custody.

Provincial police commander Supt Christopher Tamari confirmed that the arrests were made during a joint police-army operation in Malasi and nearby settlements on Saturday.

Tamari said the raid occurred around 11pm on Friday, and was perpetrated by more than 50 men from nearby communities who entered the airport area after the burial of a gang leader.

National Airports Corporation acting managing director Dominic Kaumu said the contracted security personnel on duty were outnumbered when the raid occurred.

The suspects also entered the Air Niugini and PNG Air cargo terminals, and main passenger terminal.

“The airport is not closed, however, airlines will undertake their own operational and safety assessments before confirming flight schedules,” Kaumu said.

Air Niugini has advised that its services to and from Boram Airport would be suspended until further notice.

Chief executive officer Alan Milne said: “When public service infrastructure is destroyed or damaged, it disrupts the essential flow of services to the general public, including genuine travelers who rely on us everyday.

“This senseless act of lawlessness has directly affected our ability to operate safely and efficiently into Wewak.

“Our priority now is to restore our systems and resume services as soon as it is safe to do so.”

East Sepik Governor Allan Bird urged locals to work with the police to arrest those involved.

“It is not known why Air Niugini was targeted in this brazen attack except that it may have been a target of opportunity,” he said.

“We deeply regret the damage to Air Niugini property and the inconvenience caused to the travelling public.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Chan takes governorship row to court
item.description: THE National Court is expected to hear today submissions over the governorship of New Ireland following heated arguments on Friday between two lawyers in court. New Ireland Governor Byron Chan had filed an originating summons against Namatanai MP Walter Schnaubelt, asking the court for an order to remove Schnaubelt from the office he was placed in by the provincial executive council when a vacancy
item.content:encoded:
THE National Court is expected to hear today submissions over the governorship of New Ireland following heated arguments on Friday between two lawyers in court.

New Ireland Governor Byron Chan had filed an originating summons against Namatanai MP Walter Schnaubelt, asking the court for an order to remove Schnaubelt from the office he was placed in by the provincial executive council when a vacancy existed.

Chan was declared governor after winning the recent by-election for the provincial seat left vacant by his late father, Sir Julius Chan who passed away in January last year.

Chan said Section 19 of the Organic Law on Provincial Government and Local Level Government (OLPGLLG) disqualified Schnaubelt from office once a governor was sworn in.

Chan argued that Section 17(2) of the OLPGLLG stated that a provincial member should be the governor, and that the office should not have two persons claiming it.

He said the court needed to resolve this issue as it was in the interest of the people of New Ireland.

Schnaubelt’s lawyer Rimbink Pato, argued that his client was the lawful governor, and the process of the governor-elect was the process of the provincial assembly, pursuant to sections 17(2), 19, and 20 of the OLPGLLG.

He submitted that there were two distinct offices, and that a provincial member was different.

He said there was no vacancy for the governor until it was determined by the provincial assembly, and that it was not a matter for the court to decide on.

Pato also informed the court that they had filed a Supreme Court reference on Feb 23 regarding the constitutional issue on the same, but Chan’s lawyer Alfred Manase objected saying there was no need for it as the law was clear.

Judge Pauline Bre allowed Pato an adjournment, and restrained Chan and Schnaubelt from acting as governor until today is hearing.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Proper process ensures timely services: Opa
item.description: PROPER procurement processes and principles must be adhered to for the timely delivery of goods and services to the people, says Finance Minister Thomas Opa. “It is time we improve our work and use systems that can improve work flow,” Opa said.
item.content:encoded:
PROPER procurement processes and principles must be adhered to for the timely delivery of goods and services to the people, says Finance Minister Thomas Opa.

“It is time we improve our work and use systems that can improve work flow,” Opa said.

“Public procurement must not become cumbersome, but rather promote good governance and service delivery.

“Some contracts that are awarded are held in procurement process for 18 to 24 months.

“So we need to look at reducing this lengthy process.”

He made the remarks during the launching of the National Procurement Commission (NPC) corporate plan (2026-2030), and management action plan (2026) in Port Moresby on Friday.

“The corporate plan launched today will strengthen NPC’s institutional capacity and improve procurement processes for the better,” he added.

Opa urged the NPC to implement its electronic procurement system.

“A key component of our reform agenda is the progressive adoption of electronic procurement,” he said.

“Electronic procurement must be a priority as it will promote transparency and accountability, while also reducing delays and minimising risks.

“I would also like to see the amendments of the NPC Act in my time.”

Opa also urged the NPC management and board to submit to his office plans and progress of their work to be presented in Parliament.

NPC chief executive officer Joe Sapa said the commission would focus on strengthening its current system, while also implement the electronic procurement system, now that the five-year corporate plan has been launched.

“Public procurement is a crucial for national development and service delivery,” Sapa said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Plastic waste audit training conducted
item.description: FOLLOWING the recent declaration of New Ireland’s Kafkaf village as an environmental hazard, a plastic waste audit training-of- trainers course was conducted in the province to strengthen community-driven responses to plastic pollution. The two-day training, led by the Conservation and Environment Protection Authority (Cepa) in partnership with Ailan Awareness and the Lolieng Sustainable Programme
item.content:encoded:
FOLLOWING the recent declaration of New Ireland’s Kafkaf village as an environmental hazard, a plastic waste audit training-of- trainers course was conducted in the province to strengthen community-driven responses to plastic pollution.

The two-day training, led by the Conservation and Environment Protection Authority (Cepa) in partnership with Ailan Awareness and the Lolieng Sustainable Programme, equipped six trainers with the skills and tools needed to collect plastic waste data using a mobile application.

The digital platform allows trainers to gather real-time field data using a standardised method to track waste types, quantities and sources.

Cepa consultant Ill Pilgah said the system replaces paper-based records, reducing the burden of carrying documents and printing equipment.

“It also allows for on the spot tracking, making it efficient for sea travel in between coastal islands,” he said.

The data collected would help determine key indicators such as the volume of plastic waste generated per household, the composition of waste by weight, and the extent of plastic pollution in coastal and marine environments.

Pilgah explained that the information would support local government to develop evidence-based policies, including targeted bans on certain plastic products, while also identifying gaps in waste management systems and guiding better investment and collection strategies.

He further highlighted that the audit is expected to have a strong impact on coastal communities by encouraging ownership and active participation in managing and reducing plastic pollution.

Meanwhile, Ailan Awareness representative John Aini thanked Cepa for the initiative and emphasised on the impact it would have on tourism in the province.

“This programme would motivate communities to actively participate in data-driven solutions resulting in improved environmental health and a boost for the tourism industry, the plastic waste audit encourages beach clean-ups that would restore our beaches as a major tourism attraction,” he said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: East Sepik, Manus sign agreement to boost health services
item.description: THE East Sepik Health Authority (ESPHA) has partnered with six district development authorities (DDAs) and the Manus Health Authority (MPHA) in an agreement aimed at boosting medical services across the region. Signed on Friday, the collaboration focuses on strengthening health systems and improving the quality of both primary and tertiary health services for the rural majority.
item.content:encoded:
THE East Sepik Health Authority (ESPHA) has partnered with six district development authorities (DDAs) and the Manus Health Authority (MPHA) in an agreement aimed at boosting medical services across the region.

Signed on Friday, the collaboration focuses on strengthening health systems and improving the quality of both primary and tertiary health services for the rural majority.

Health secretary Pascoe Kase highlighted that these service-level agreements (SLAs) were a vital tool for financial accountability.

“We sign this agreement and it details exactly what that money is used for in health in a particular district,” Kase said.

He noted that 20 per cent of district resources were legally meant for health delivery, and these agreements ensured funds were spent on targeted areas rather than in an ad hoc manner.

ESPHA chief executive officer Mathew Kaluvia described the signing as a “deliberate step” toward closer alignment of the provincial health system.

He emphasised that the SLAs clarified duties and expectations, paving the way for better responses to epidemics and natural disasters.

“The SLA is the way forward to improve health service delivery in the province,” Kaluvia added, noting that DDAs look after the majority of the population at the district level.

The partnership includes a unique memorandum of agreement (MoA) with Manus to assist remote island communities.

Under this arrangement, ESPHA will cater to patients from the Wuvulu and Western islands and release medical specialists to Manus, provided MPHA covers the operational costs, including a K2,000 monthly fee for patients accommodated at Boram Hospital. Manus Executive Council’s health chairman Hans Poiou explained that the Western islands were geographically closer to East Sepik, making logistics difficult.

“It is good that we sign an agreement between us so we can help each other,” Poiou said.

East Sepik Governor Allan Bird attributed the progress to consistent management, which recently secured K50 million funding from the World Bank for new facilities.

“I want to thank our various DDAs who have recognised that and are now going to sign those agreements,” Governor Bird said.

International Trade and Investment Minister and member for Yangoru Saussia Richard Maru confirmed that the Yangoru-Saussia DDA was already acting on the agreement, with plans to build six new health centres and a Level Four district hospital.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Woman balances motherhood and beauty business
item.description: KELLIANNE Ewam is balancing time between motherhood, a full-time career in marketing and running a business called Willions Boutiques. Ewam, 34, from Morobe, and the only female in a family of eight, is starting a nail technology service through her beauty business.
item.content:encoded:
KELLIANNE Ewam is balancing time between motherhood, a full-time career in marketing and running a business called Willions Boutiques.

Ewam, 34, from Morobe, and the only female in a family of eight, is starting a nail technology service through her beauty business.

She had wanted to become a television presenter or journalist but ended up in a marketing career.

She has an Australian degree in international marketing and has worked for more than 12 years as a marketing professional.

Before starting her nail technology business, Ewam does business at home during her free time.

She did online sales of fashion items, mainly babies’ and ladies’ accessories, on lay-by terms depending on demand.  

Ewam recently started making skin care essential oils moisturisers and said she believes in looking good and feeling good.

“The way I look influences how I feel,” she said.

“I am a self-starter, I always look for opportunities to be better than my former self. Life has challenged me in so many ways, and I believe it has also motivated, inspired me to venture into business.”

Ewam discovered nail technology around 2021 in Wanchai, Hong Kong by a Chinese Malay nail technician. Layer, she tried out services at salons and spas in Port Moresby.

She bought her own self-kit from overseas and began practising.

“So I was self-taught, with the experiences I have here in PNG and overseas have gave me confidence to venture,” she said.

It was Ewam’s husband who encouraged her to venture into nail technology service.

 “I am always grateful if it wasn’t for him, we will never come this far,” she said.

The business was registered in February 2024. After almost a year of mobile services, she signed a lease with Monian Limited for an SME space inside Garden City, Boroko. Today, her customers range from 10-year-old girls to women over 50. Despite the challenges and the competition from other SMEs, Ewam focuses on customer satisfaction.

“I don’t worry about competition, I worry about where or not my customers are satisfied and their Kina pays for quality,” she said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Water PNG gets K220mil loan to expand water supply
item.description: THE Asian Development Bank (ADB) is giving Water PNG Limited a US$60.9 million (about K220 million) loan package to expand and modernise water supply and sanitation services in Papua New Guinea. Water PNG Limited chief executive officer James Young said that the investment was a demonstration of confidence in the government’s Reset@50 strategy to correct the past and secure the future.
item.content:encoded:
THE Asian Development Bank (ADB) is giving Water PNG Limited a US$60.9 million (about K220 million) loan package to expand and modernise water supply and sanitation services in Papua New Guinea.

Water PNG Limited chief executive officer James Young said that the investment was a demonstration of confidence in the government’s Reset@50 strategy to correct the past and secure the future.

“This investment is the ADB’s first water sector loan to PNG in over 25 years, signalling renewed confidence in the country’s water service transformation agenda,” Young said.

“The Government has made it clear that State-owned enterprises have to move onwards and not dwell in failures of the past to keep pace with a growing population.

“We have our plans to replace water and sewage infrastructure that is half a century old and we have the full support of the government and partners to deliver on this rehabilitation,” he said.

The financing package comprises US$30 million (about K124 million) in standard capital resources, US$30.9 million (about K128.1 million) in concessional resources, US$3 million (about K12.4 million) in counterpart funding from the government, and an extra US$1.5 million (about K6.2 million) in technical assistance.

Young said that the funds would facilitate digital systems integration, strengthen institutions and explore public–private partnerships.

“This investment marks a transformative moment for our urban communities,” he said.

“Access to safe, reliable water is a fundamental human right, and the ADB’s support allows Water PNG to fast-track long-overdue upgrades to our systems.

“We welcome ADB’s confidence in Water PNG, especially its commitment to helping us modernise through digital integration, institutional strengthening and exploring innovative partnerships.”

Meanwhile Young said this funding sets the foundation for a more resilient, efficient and sustainable water future for the country.

He said that the Urban Water Supply and Sanitation Security and Resilience Improvement Project was projected to benefit over 160,000 Papua New Guineans.

“Planned upgrades involve rehabilitating wastewater systems, enhancing piped water access, and bolstering utility operations to improve public health,” he said.

“Water PNG is dedicated to ensuring successful implementation aligned with national WaSH goals and the Government’s Medium-Term Development Plan.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Tisa Bank to launch Chinese Yuan account
item.description: FOREIGN Affairs Minister Justin Tkatchenko says the move by Tisa Bank to launch the Chinese Yuan Account is in line with diplomatic efforts by the Foreign Affairs Department.  He said that the move to offer direct settlement for the currency was good news for the economy.
item.content:encoded:
FOREIGN Affairs Minister Justin Tkatchenko says the move by Tisa Bank to launch the Chinese Yuan Account is in line with diplomatic efforts by the Foreign Affairs Department. 

He said that the move to offer direct settlement for the currency was good news for the economy.

“This is also to make business transactions easier for its customers,” he said.

Tkatchenko said that China was the biggest economic and trading partner and the initiative would further facilitate the relationship.

“This is among efforts that the Department of Foreign Affairs was putting in place for the benefit of both PNG and China,” he said.

“The Chinese business community in PNG is also pleased with this service as it will make it more accessible, cheaper and easier for them in terms of procurement and investments.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Mama Bank, Nakanai authority to help women
item.description: THE Women’s Micro Bank (Mama Bank) and the Nakanai development authority have signed an agreement to help women in small-medium enterprises (SMEs) access finance. Nakanai MP Francis Maneke said women and youths in the district should have access to banking facilities and have their own accounts with affordable and adequate access to funds to enable them to play a more active role in the economic d
item.content:encoded:
THE Women’s Micro Bank (Mama Bank) and the Nakanai development authority have signed an agreement to help women in small-medium enterprises (SMEs) access finance.

Nakanai MP Francis Maneke said women and youths in the district should have access to banking facilities and have their own accounts with affordable and adequate access to funds to enable them to play a more active role in the economic development of the district.

He said during the signing ceremony in Port Moresby last week that the Mama Bank would use the previous funding of K500,000 and a further K500,000 for the purpose of MSME and SME access to finance funding.

Mama Bank chief executive officer Gunanidhi Das said the Mama Bank would provide financial inclusion with account opening, easy and affordable savings products, appropriate and easily accessible small loans to informal entrepreneurs to start or improve existing small income-generating business enterprises.

Nakanai DDA chief executive officer Jeffery Osa said the programme would support “mothers selling fresh market produce in the markets in Nakanai District and markets on the road side, simple people”.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.
